design_philosophy.core_loop: Tetris: rotate and place falling tetrominoes to form complete horizontal lines without gaps; completed lines clear and blocks fall. Dr. Mario: rotate and drop colored pills to match and eliminate viruses in sets of two or more same-colored pieces horizontally or vertically.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure and spatial constraint: managing falling pieces/pills while board fills toward game-over line at top of screen.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Puzzle mastery through pattern recognition and spatial reasoning; competitive puzzle excellence in multiplayer.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Dual-game compilation strategy emphasizing multiplayer as core value proposition. The 'Mixed Match' mode—which transitions between Tetris and Dr. Mario games during single competitive session—is the defining opinionated design choice, treating two traditionally separate puzzle games as complementary experiences rather than isolated options. This design stance won critical and commercial praise, suggesting developers believed puzzle diversity within one session enhanced engagement over single-game depth.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Players can customize difficulty level, time limit, and music before games begin in Mixed Match mode. Game-over occurs when pieces/pills reach top of screen—no configurable lives; failure is immediate and resets.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Dr. Mario stages progress sequentially; completing all viruses on a stage advances to next stage with increased virus count/complexity. Tetris modes (A-Type, B-Type) progress by level/lines cleared.
replayability_hooks: Mixed Match mode forces players to alternate between two games, creating novel strategic combinations
Head-to-head multiplayer competitive modes for both Tetris and Dr. Mario
Customizable difficulty, time limits, and music before session start
Two distinct game mechanics (line-clearing vs. virus-matching) within single compilation
